tossups.question: Alfred Hitchcock interrupted this director’s lunch with John Ford to compliment him on a scene that reveals the heroine’s amputation, with Hitchcock saying “ah, that leg, that leg.” Roger Vivier’s (“roh-ZHAY viv-YAY’s”) metal buckle pump shoes were popularized by the star of a film by this director whose title inspired Brooke Magnanti’s pen name. William Friedkin’s casting agent got him the wrong actor from a film by this director to play the villain of The (*) French Connection. In a 1967 film, this director used the recurring sounds of horse hooves and jingling bells to symbolize a woman’s sexual desires that her doctor husband cannot satisfy. Jean-Claude Carrière (“car-YAIR”) co-wrote that film by this director, whose title puns on the daytime liaisons of the heroine, a sexually repressed housewife. For 10 points, Catherine Deneuve (“duh-NUV”) starred in what director’s films Tristana and Belle de Jour?
tossups.question_sanitized: Alfred Hitchcock interrupted this director's lunch with John Ford to compliment him on a scene that reveals the heroine's amputation, with Hitchcock saying "ah, that leg, that leg." Roger Vivier's ("roh-ZHAY viv-YAY's") metal buckle pump shoes were popularized by the star of a film by this director whose title inspired Brooke Magnanti's pen name. William Friedkin's casting agent got him the wrong actor from a film by this director to play the villain of The (*) French Connection. In a 1967 film, this director used the recurring sounds of horse hooves and jingling bells to symbolize a woman's sexual desires that her doctor husband cannot satisfy. Jean-Claude Carriere ("car-YAIR") co-wrote that film by this director, whose title puns on the daytime liaisons of the heroine, a sexually repressed housewife. For 10 points, Catherine Deneuve ("duh-NUV") starred in what director's films Tristana and Belle de Jour?
tossups.answer: Luis Buñuel(“boon-yoo-EL”) [or Luis Buñuel Portolés] (Brooke Magnanti wrote The Intimate Adventures of a London Call Girl using the pseudonym Belle de Jour. Friedkin meant to cast Paco Rabal after asking his agent to “get that Spanish actor from Belle de Jour,” but the agent got Fernando Rey instead.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Luis Bunuel("boon-yoo-EL") [or Luis Bunuel Portoles] (Brooke Magnanti wrote The Intimate Adventures of a London Call Girl using the pseudonym Belle de Jour. Friedkin meant to cast Paco Rabal after asking his agent to "get that Spanish actor from Belle de Jour," but the agent got Fernando Rey instead.)

tossups.question: The protagonist of a film by this director compares the people below to worms while he stands in a bell tower and contemplates strangling his wife. In that film by this director, Francisco falls in love with Gloria at first sight when he spots her at a foot-washing ceremony. Audiences were incensed by a scene in which this director had a character in a reformatory school throw an egg at the camera so that the yolk ran down the lens. (*) Pedro’s body is thrown onto a pile of garbage at the end of that film by this director about children living in a slum in Mexico City. Él and Los Olvidados (“lohss ol-vee-DAH-dohss”) are among the films this director made in Mexico in the 50s. For 10 points, name this Spanish-born director of The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie and Un Chien Andalou (“un shyen ahn-dah-LOO”).
tossups.question_sanitized: The protagonist of a film by this director compares the people below to worms while he stands in a bell tower and contemplates strangling his wife. In that film by this director, Francisco falls in love with Gloria at first sight when he spots her at a foot-washing ceremony. Audiences were incensed by a scene in which this director had a character in a reformatory school throw an egg at the camera so that the yolk ran down the lens. (*) Pedro's body is thrown onto a pile of garbage at the end of that film by this director about children living in a slum in Mexico City. El and Los Olvidados ("lohss ol-vee-DAH-dohss") are among the films this director made in Mexico in the 50s. For 10 points, name this Spanish-born director of The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie and Un Chien Andalou ("un shyen ahn-dah-LOO").

tossups.question: In lieu of hiring another musician, David Bowie simply screened a film by this director for the opening act of his 1976 tour promoting Station to Station. In a film by this director, an instrumental version of the Liebestod [LEE-bus-tote] plays as a woman kisses the face of a fallen cyclist and takes a striped box from around his neck. For that film, this director interspersed music from Tristan und Isolde with Argentine (*) tangos, an effect he created by hiding behind the screen and alternating between two different records; that story is related in this director’s autobiography My Last Sigh. A song based on this director’s work replaced a word ending with “ou” [oo] with one ending with “usia” [OOS-yuh] because the songwriter thought it sounded “too French”; that song opens the album Doolittle. For 10 points, name this director whose most famous work inspired the Pixies song “Debaser,” which repeats “slicing up eyeballs, I want you to know” and “I am un chien andalusia” [oon sh’yen ahn-dah-LOOSE-yuh].
tossups.question_sanitized: In lieu of hiring another musician, David Bowie simply screened a film by this director for the opening act of his 1976 tour promoting Station to Station. In a film by this director, an instrumental version of the Liebestod [LEE-bus-tote] plays as a woman kisses the face of a fallen cyclist and takes a striped box from around his neck. For that film, this director interspersed music from Tristan und Isolde with Argentine (*) tangos, an effect he created by hiding behind the screen and alternating between two different records; that story is related in this director's autobiography My Last Sigh. A song based on this director's work replaced a word ending with "ou" [oo] with one ending with "usia" [OOS-yuh] because the songwriter thought it sounded "too French"; that song opens the album Doolittle. For 10 points, name this director whose most famous work inspired the Pixies song "Debaser," which repeats "slicing up eyeballs, I want you to know" and "I am un chien andalusia" [oon sh'yen ahn-dah-LOOSE-yuh].

tossups.question: A book titled The Unspeakable Betrayal compiles writings of this director, including a 1959 essay in which he wrote about the lack of a necessary amount of mystery in film. This director began one film with a montage that tilts up on the Eiffel Tower while a voice-over describes problems that plague “almost every capital in the world”. A film by this director, which caused a riot that saw far-right Frenchman destroy a group of paintings, features a shot of several scalps on a cross, and begins with close-ups on (*) scorpions paired with intertitles describing their life. In a film by this director, the walls fall from the set as dining characters realize they are being observed by a live audience. A scene created by this director, often interpreted as an allegory for the weight of cultural mores, sees a character drag two men and pianos behind himself before a close-up on a hand ridden with ants. For 10 points, identify this Spanish director, a pioneering surrealist who collaborated with Salvador Dali on an iconic short film that features the slicing of an eyeball.
tossups.answer: Luis Buñuel Portolés [The other films are Los Olivadados, The Age of Gold, and The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Luis Bunuel Portoles [The other films are Los Olivadados, The Age of Gold, and The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie]
tossups.question_sanitized: A book titled The Unspeakable Betrayal compiles writings of this director, including a 1959 essay in which he wrote about the lack of a necessary amount of mystery in film. This director began one film with a montage that tilts up on the Eiffel Tower while a voice-over describes problems that plague "almost every capital in the world". A film by this director, which caused a riot that saw far-right Frenchman destroy a group of paintings, features a shot of several scalps on a cross, and begins with close-ups on (*) scorpions paired with intertitles describing their life. In a film by this director, the walls fall from the set as dining characters realize they are being observed by a live audience. A scene created by this director, often interpreted as an allegory for the weight of cultural mores, sees a character drag two men and pianos behind himself before a close-up on a hand ridden with ants. For 10 points, identify this Spanish director, a pioneering surrealist who collaborated with Salvador Dali on an iconic short film that features the slicing of an eyeball.

tossups.question: When an assistant told this director that the setting he was using for a recurring shot of six people walking silently down a country road had been used in ten other films, he replied “Then it must be good.” In a film by this director, a man buys a dog after seeing it cruelly tied to a cart, then walks off the road just as another cart with a dog tied to it drives by. In that film by him, a leper puts a phonograph of Handel’s “Hallelujah Chorus,” leading to a dance party that ends with the characters lining up on the side of a table, Last Supper-style, for a photograph. The title would-be (*) nun of a film by this director turns her uncle’s estate into a home for beggars. This director is the alphabetically first of two men who worked on a film in which a man laboriously pulls a piano containing two decaying donkeys into the room. For 10 points, name this director of The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie and Viridiana who collaborated with Salvador Dalí on Un chien andalou.
tossups.question_sanitized: When an assistant told this director that the setting he was using for a recurring shot of six people walking silently down a country road had been used in ten other films, he replied "Then it must be good." In a film by this director, a man buys a dog after seeing it cruelly tied to a cart, then walks off the road just as another cart with a dog tied to it drives by. In that film by him, a leper puts a phonograph of Handel's "Hallelujah Chorus," leading to a dance party that ends with the characters lining up on the side of a table, Last Supper-style, for a photograph. The title would-be (*) nun of a film by this director turns her uncle's estate into a home for beggars. This director is the alphabetically first of two men who worked on a film in which a man laboriously pulls a piano containing two decaying donkeys into the room. For 10 points, name this director of The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie and Viridiana who collaborated with Salvador Dali on Un chien andalou.

tossups.question: In one of this man’s works, a broken window is casually blamed on “some Jew passing by” even though a person inside the house broke it. This man showed several people smashing a wall to reach a water pipe in a film inspired partly by Sartre’s No Exit, set in a bourgeois music room. Another film directed by this non-Pasolini man showed a man slapping a woman’s face for spilling wine on his hand, ends with “120 Days of Depraved Acts,” and depicts a couple played by Gaston Modot and Lya Lys getting repeatedly (*) sexually thwarted. This director of The Exterminating Angel was the alphabetically-prior collaborator on a short film in which ants crawl out of a person’s hand and a razor slits a woman’s eyeball. For 10 points, name this director of L’Age d’Or, who worked with fellow Spaniard Salvador Dalí on Un chien andalou.
tossups.question_sanitized: In one of this man's works, a broken window is casually blamed on "some Jew passing by" even though a person inside the house broke it. This man showed several people smashing a wall to reach a water pipe in a film inspired partly by Sartre's No Exit, set in a bourgeois music room. Another film directed by this non-Pasolini man showed a man slapping a woman's face for spilling wine on his hand, ends with "120 Days of Depraved Acts," and depicts a couple played by Gaston Modot and Lya Lys getting repeatedly (*) sexually thwarted. This director of The Exterminating Angel was the alphabetically-prior collaborator on a short film in which ants crawl out of a person's hand and a razor slits a woman's eyeball. For 10 points, name this director of L'Age d'Or, who worked with fellow Spaniard Salvador Dali on Un chien andalou.

bonuses.leadin: Answer some questions about the film career of actress Catherine Deneuve. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer some questions about the film career of actress Catherine Deneuve. For 10 points each:
parts: Deneuve is most famous for her collaborations with Jacques Demy during this cinematic movement. In a film from this movement, a Humphrey Bogart-obsessed character kills a cop after being caught with a stolen car.

Deneuve plays a woman who turns to prostitution to fulfill masochistic fantasies in this director’s film Belle de Jour. This man worked with Salvador Dali in his debut, Un Chien Andalou (“ahn SHYEN ahn-DAH-loo”).

In a film inspired by the Dogme (“DAW-muh”) 95 movement, Deneuve plays a woman who describes Hollywood musicals to a blind character played by this actress. This actress secured an Academy Award nomination for her song “I’ve Seen It All.”
parts_sanitized: Deneuve is most famous for her collaborations with Jacques Demy during this cinematic movement. In a film from this movement, a Humphrey Bogart-obsessed character kills a cop after being caught with a stolen car.

Deneuve plays a woman who turns to prostitution to fulfill masochistic fantasies in this director's film Belle de Jour. This man worked with Salvador Dali in his debut, Un Chien Andalou ("ahn SHYEN ahn-DAH-loo").

In a film inspired by the Dogme ("DAW-muh") 95 movement, Deneuve plays a woman who describes Hollywood musicals to a blind character played by this actress. This actress secured an Academy Award nomination for her song "I've Seen It All."
answers: French New Wave [accept nouvelle vague] (The unnamed film is Breathless.)
answers_sanitized: French New Wave [accept nouvelle vague] (The unnamed film is Breathless.)

bonuses.leadin: In a film, the protagonist is told that this character is fake by her sister Isabel, who urges her to communicate with this character using her mind. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In a film, the protagonist is told that this character is fake by her sister Isabel, who urges her to communicate with this character using her mind. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this character who fascinates the young girl Ana when she sees him in a film brought to her village by a mobile cinema. After running away from home to a forest, Ana is approached by this character beside a lake.
Frankenstein appears in The Spirit of the Beehive, a film from this country. In a film by a director from this country, a group of friends attempt to dine together but are constantly interrupted.
The latter film, The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie, is by this Spanish director who collaborated with Salvador Dalí on the experimental silent film Un Chien Andalou.

The latter film, The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie, is by this Spanish director who collaborated with Salvador Dali on the experimental silent film Un Chien Andalou.

bonuses.leadin: Jay Rosenblatt’s short The Smell of Burning Ants incorporates documentary footage about these animals committing suicide. For 10 points each:
parts: Name these animals, which are attacked by a swarm of ants as children look on in a symbolic early scene from The Wild Bunch.
Soldiers cheer on two scorpions fighting to the death in a scene from this 2005 Sam Mendes film about Marines in the Gulf War.
Documentary footage of scorpions opens L’Age d’Or (“lahzh dor”), the second surrealistic collaboration between Salvador Dalí and this director, after Un Chien Andalou (“un shee-AN ahn-dah-LOO”).

bonuses.leadin: This composer worked with Australian playwright Meredith Oakes on an operatic adaptation of The Tempest that premiered in 2005. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this contemporary British composer, whose opera Powder Her Face contains a notorious musical depiction of fellatio. His other works include America: A Prophecy.

Powder Her Face is a chamber opera, a term that this other composer applied to his The Rape of Lucretia and his opera in which the words “the ceremony of innocence is drowned” are sung by a spirit haunting Miles and the Governess.
Adès’s opera The Exterminating Angel adapts a film by a man from this European country. It is the setting for Georges Bizet’s Carmen.
answers: Benjamin Britten (the opera is The Turn of the Screw)
(Kingdom of) Spain (the film is by Luis Buñuel)
answers_sanitized: Benjamin Britten (the opera is The Turn of the Screw)
(Kingdom of) Spain (the film is by Luis Bunuel)

bonuses.leadin: The opening track of this album, talks of “slicing up eyeballs”. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this album which includes such tracks as "Monkey Gone to Heaven", "Gouge Away" and the aforementioned "Debaser"?
In talking about slicing up eyeballs, "Debaser" makes reference to Un chien Andalou, the debut work by which highly influential Spanish filmmaker?
This 1972 film by Buñuel inspired an episode of One Foot in the Grave in which the characters become locked in a conservatory
answers_sanitized: El Angel Exterminador (accept The Exterminating Angel)

bonuses.leadin: This film opens with documentary footage of scorpions. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this film in which two lover's attempts to consummate their relationship are thwarted by bourgeois French society. This surrealist comedy includes a scene in which a woman erotically sucks on the toes of a religious statue.
This man directed, and worked with Salvador Dali on the screenplay for, L'Age d'Or. The two collaborated on another movie in which a young man dresses in a nun's outfit.
This aforementioned Buñuel/Dali collaboration opens with the director cutting an eye with a razor. At one point in this film, ants crawl out of a young man's hand.
answers: L'Age d'Or [or The Age of Gold; or The Golden Age]
answers_sanitized: L'Age d'Or [or The Age of Gold; or The Golden Age]
Un chien andalou [or An Andalusian Dog]
